title: Tries (Prefix Trees)
description: Master trie data structure for efficient string operations

documentation.content:
# Tries: The Autocomplete Superpower

Imagine typing "app" in a search box and instantly seeing "apple", "application", "appointment". How does it find all words starting with "app" so fast? The answer is a trie (pronounced "try")—a tree where each path from root to leaf spells out a word.

## The Story Behind Tries

A trie, also called a prefix tree, is a tree data structure used for storing and retrieving strings efficiently. Each node represents a character, and paths from root to nodes spell out prefixes. It's called a trie because it's used for reTRIEval.

The genius of tries: common prefixes are stored once. "app", "apple", and "application" share the path a→p→p.

## The Structure

Each trie node contains:
- **Children**: Map/array of child nodes (one per character)
- **isEndOfWord**: Boolean marking if this node completes a word
- **Optional**: Count, value, or other metadata

## When to Use Tries

**Autocomplete** - Find all words with given prefix
**Spell checker** - Check if word exists, suggest corrections
**IP routing** - Longest prefix matching
**Dictionary operations** - Insert, search, delete words
**Word games** - Boggle, Scrabble word validation
**Phone directory** - T9 predictive text

## Core Operations

**Insert word** - Create path for each character: O(m) where m = word length
**Search word** - Follow path, check isEndOfWord: O(m)
**Search prefix** - Follow path, don't need isEndOfWord: O(m)
**Delete word** - Mark isEndOfWord false, optionally prune: O(m)

## The Performance

**Time**: O(m) for insert/search/delete where m = word length
**Space**: O(ALPHABET_SIZE * N * M) where N = number of words, M = average length

Tries trade space for speed—they use more memory but give you blazing fast lookups!

## Common Mistakes

❌ **Not marking end of word** - Can't distinguish "app" from "apple" prefix
❌ **Wrong alphabet size** - 26 for lowercase, 52 for mixed case, 256 for ASCII
❌ **Not handling empty string** - Edge case that breaks many implementations
❌ **Memory leaks** - In languages without GC, must free nodes properly
❌ **Inefficient child storage** - Array vs hash map trade-off

## Pro Tips

💡 **Array vs HashMap for children** - Array for small alphabet (26), HashMap for large/sparse
💡 **Store count at nodes** - Track how many words pass through each node
💡 **Lazy deletion** - Just mark isEndOfWord false, don't remove nodes
💡 **Compressed tries** - Merge single-child chains to save space
💡 **Ternary search tries** - Space-efficient variant

## Real-World Applications

- **Autocomplete** - Search engines, IDEs, mobile keyboards
- **Spell checkers** - Word validation and suggestions
- **IP routing** - Longest prefix matching in routers
- **Genome analysis** - DNA sequence matching
- **Text editors** - Word completion, syntax highlighting
- **Phone contacts** - T9 predictive text

Tries are the secret sauce behind autocomplete and fast string operations. They're space-hungry but incredibly fast for prefix-based operations! 🌳🔤

questions.question: What is the time complexity of searching for a word of length m in a trie?
questions.explanation: Searching in a trie takes O(m) time where m is the length of the word, independent of the number of words stored.

questions.question: What does each node in a trie represent?
questions.explanation: Each node in a trie represents a single character. Paths from root to nodes represent prefixes.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of tries over hash maps?
questions.explanation: Tries excel at prefix-based operations like autocomplete, which hash maps cannot do efficiently.

questions.question: How do you mark that a word ends at a trie node?
questions.explanation: A boolean flag (isEndOfWord) marks nodes where complete words end, distinguishing words from prefixes.

problems.title: Implement Trie (Prefix Tree)
problems.description: A trie (pronounced as try) or prefix tree is a tree data structure used to efficiently store and retrieve keys in a dataset of strings. Implement the Trie class with insert, search, and startsWith methods.
constraints: word and prefix consist only of lowercase English letters
At most 30000 calls in total will be made to insert, search, and startsWith

constraints: board[i][j] is a lowercase English letter

exampleTestCases.description: Find words that exist in board

commonMistakes: Not marking end of word - cannot distinguish words from prefixes
Wrong alphabet size - 26 for lowercase only, adjust for other character sets
Not handling empty strings - edge case that breaks many implementations
Memory leaks in C/C++ - must properly free all nodes
Using array when HashMap is better - array wastes space for sparse alphabets
